| T-022 Electoral Cycle Capture | Add administrative hollowing tests and explicit acknowledgement that constitutional design can raise the cost of repeal but cannot fully prevent determined political reversal. | [OECD Public Integrity Indicators](https://www.oecd-ilibrary.org/en/topics/public-integrity.html) show why implementation and institutional capacity gaps matter after rules exist. | Transition-of-power simulation, budget-starvation scenario, vacant-office trigger test, independent-body legitimacy review. | Threat Register, Claims and Evidence Register, Pilot Evidence Roadmap. |
| T-023 Pilot External Validity Collapse | Require substitute evidence where real crises cannot be ethically induced; failed pilots cannot be rebranded as success. | [Sphere Handbook](https://spherestandards.org/handbook/) anchors emergency minimums; OECD integrity sources anchor adversarial governance and implementation gaps. | Stress-evidence dossier, independent challenge window, crisis simulation not previously rehearsed. | Pilot Evidence Roadmap, Claims and Evidence Register, Threat Register. |
| T-025 Investment and Capital-Deployment Shelter Capture | Treat long-horizon capital deployment as a capture surface: project parking, force-majeure abuse, procurement corruption, escrow delay, and legal-wrapper avoidance must be tested under the CRUS fiscal architecture. | [OECD procurement integrity](https://www.oecd.org/en/topics/sub-issues/integrity-in-public-procurement.html) and [FATF beneficial ownership guidance](https://www.fatf-gafi.org/content/fatf-gafi/en/publications/Fatfrecommendations/Guidance-Beneficial-Ownership-Legal-Persons.html) anchor procurement and hidden-control risks. Historical demurrage sources remain useful only as superseded design context. | Project-finance simulation, procurement-integrity red team, beneficial-ownership/legal-wrapper review, source-base avoidance review, the [Commons Return and Universal Stake Evidence Test Package](./Commons_Return_Universal_Stake_Evidence_Test_Package.md), and the [Anti-Rent Legal Wrapper Evidence Test Package](./Anti_Rent_Legal_Wrapper_Evidence_Test_Package.md). | Threat Register, Threat Resolution Matrix, Claims and Evidence Register, Pilot Evidence Roadmap, Patch Log. |
| T-029 Fiscal Sustainability and Currency Debasement | Treat the funding model as a first-order threat: every scale claim must show what funds the survival floor, public rails, governance, resilience reserves, and Universal Stake without inflation, hidden debt, prohibited-base migration, or downward burden shift. | Public-finance history, inflation/debasement failures, resource-revenue volatility, land-value taxation debates, and sovereign-wealth-fund governance all show that funding mechanisms fail when source bases are overestimated, politically captured, or shifted to weaker households. | Fiscal adequacy model, Flow issuance/debasement tolerance, remaining-tax necessity review, Essential Access lockbox sufficiency test, source-base valuation red team, incidence/dignity review, and public "who pays" comprehension test. | Threat Register T-029, Threat Resolution Matrix, Claims and Evidence Register, Hardening Queue, Pilot Evidence Roadmap, Patch Log, Annex D. |
| T-030 Cyber Resilience and Availability Failure | Treat digital survival-floor availability as a first-order threat distinct from tamper evidence and oracle failure: the floor must remain reachable when systems, keys, networks, providers, or software supply chains fail. | NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0, NIST SP 800-34, NIST SP 800-57, and CISA ransomware guidance anchor the need for tested recovery, contingency planning, key management, incident response, and offline backup discipline. | Critical-service inventory, ransomware continuity drill, emergency key-revocation and rotation test, offline/analog redemption test, regional failover drill, provider-settlement continuity, vulnerable-cohort continuity report, public-status fallback, and supply-chain implant red team. | Threat Register T-030, Threat Resolution Matrix, Claims and Evidence Register, Hardening Queue, Pilot Evidence Roadmap, Cyber Resilience and Availability Evidence Test Package. |
| T-031 Last-Resort Unenrolled Access Failure | Treat the never-enrolled person as a first-order delivery threat distinct from identity recovery: the floor must remain reachable without enrollment, wallet, digital credential, stable address, phone, biometric, future enrollment promise, or identity-disclosing intermediary. | NIST SP 800-63-4, World Bank ID4D, UNHCR registration guidance, and Sphere standards anchor the reality that identity systems and service access fail when practical barriers and humanitarian minimums are ignored. | No-credential intake drill, trusted-intermediary route test, analog reconciliation privacy audit, abuse/diversion drill, provider-settlement continuity, vulnerable-cohort disparity review, abandonment accounting, and dignity interviews. | Threat Register T-031, Threat Resolution Matrix, Claims and Evidence Register, Hardening Queue, Pilot Evidence Roadmap, Last-Resort Unenrolled Access Evidence Test Package. |
| T-032 Monitoring Repurposing and Enforcement-Observation Failure | Treat monitoring itself as a first-order threat: every enforcement, dashboard, identity, delivery, provider, oracle, Service Record, cyber, and anti-fraud data stream must be purpose-bound, minimized, appealable, independently reviewed, retention-limited, and tested for repurposing. | OECD public-integrity sources, privacy-by-design practice, NIST identity guidance, and the corpus's own Capture Dashboard and Annex C controls show that monitoring and accountability systems fail when implementation, access roles, and secondary use are not tested. | Monitoring Purpose Register, purpose-creep red team, linkability/re-identification test, office-separation drill, individual-flag appeal drill, data-minimization and retention audit, and coercive-use scenario. | Threat Register T-032, Threat Resolution Matrix, Claims and Evidence Register, Hardening Queue, Pilot Evidence Roadmap, Monitoring Repurposing Evidence Test Package, Capture Dashboard Specification, Annex C. |
| T-033 Founding Consent Evidence and Civil-Society Review Definition Failure | Treat founding consent evidence and civil-society review as first-order legitimacy gates: binding consent must follow the Founding Order threshold, and review must come from qualified bodies with no dependence on founders, funders, vendors, or pilot administration. | Ostrom commons evidence and OECD public-integrity sources anchor the importance of local consent, affected-community accountability, conflict disclosure, and independent review. | Founding Consent and Civil-Society Review Evidence Test Package; threshold certification, opt-out proof, aid-nonconditioning proof, pressure survey, coercion complaint log, exit-cost report, reviewer qualification register, public challenge log, and separate independent findings. | Threat Register T-033, Threat Resolution Matrix, Claims and Evidence Register, Hardening Queue, Pilot Evidence Roadmap, Founding Legitimacy Dossier. |
| Essential-sector conglomerate refusal | Treat oil, energy, medicine, logistics, and PBM-style intermediaries as survival-leverage actors, not just ordinary vendors. | IEA energy-investment data, EIA petroleum/electricity dependence data, FTC PBM concentration findings, CMS health-spending data, OECD procurement scale, OpenSecrets lobbying data, WHO medicine-access warnings, ASHP shortage data, NREL transformer bottleneck evidence, NARUC utility-regulation history, and FATF beneficial-ownership guidance. | [Conglomerate Transition Dossier](./Conglomerate_Transition_Dossier.md) and [Essential-Sector Refusal Test Package](./Essential_Sector_Refusal_Test_Package.md); largest-supplier exit drill, medicine/PBM refusal drill, grid/logistics delay drill, medicine stockpile audit, energy reserve test, public fallback capacity model, compliant-bidder count, beneficial-owner trace, and lobbying/capture exposure review. | Annex AT, Annex AR, Claims and Evidence Register, Hardening Queue, Pilot Evidence Roadmap, Capture Dashboard Specification, Parameter Calibration Register. |
| Implementation drift and hard-lock bypass | Treat code/config/corpus drift as a control-plane threat, not an engineering detail. | NIST FIPS 180-4, RFC 6962, NISTIR 8214, NIST SP 800-57, and timelock practitioner patterns anchor the integrity mechanisms and their limits. | [Implementation Drift Audit Package](./Implementation_Drift_Audit_Package.md); reproducible hash test, startup refusal drill, keyholder review, timelock response drill, supply-chain bypass review. | Architecture files, INVARIANTS, Claims and Evidence Register, Capture Dashboard Specification. |

Read the partial threats through the collapse-state lens in the [Architecture Source Map](./Architecture_Source_Map.md): T-001, T-002, T-006, T-015, T-019, and T-024 primarily guard against Survival-Trade Bind; T-007, T-016, T-017, T-022, and T-025 pressure Power-Wealth Convergence; T-004, T-008, T-009, and T-027 pressure Static-Advantage Loop; and T-023 can falsely certify all three if calm pilots are mistaken for field evidence.

---

## Load-Bearing Sub-Problems

| Sub-problem | Structural resolution path | Outside evidence anchor | Required proof before stronger status | Documents to update |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Identity-stack fraud calibration | Bind identity rules to published asymmetric error thresholds, recovery timing, coercion safeguards, staff-discretion limits, and appeal timing. | NIST SP 800-63-4; World Bank ID4D; UNHCR registration guidance; W3C Verifiable Credentials. | Identity and Recovery Evidence Test Package; false-exclusion and recovery-delay pilot by vulnerable population group. | Threat Register T-002; Claims and Evidence Register; Pilot Evidence Roadmap. |
| Last-resort floor for the never-enrolled | Bind universality claims to a route that works before identity, before a wallet, before a digital credential, and before ordinary administration. | NIST SP 800-63-4; World Bank ID4D; UNHCR registration guidance; Sphere standards. | Last-Resort Unenrolled Access Evidence Test Package; no-credential and trusted-intermediary drills with privacy and dignity review. | Threat Register T-031; Claims and Evidence Register; Pilot Evidence Roadmap; Hardening Queue. |
| Monitoring purpose limitation | Bind enforcement claims to proof that monitoring streams cannot be purpose-crept, joined, re-identified, self-certified, retained indefinitely, or used for coercion. | OECD integrity sources; NIST identity guidance; Capture Dashboard Specification; Annex C. | Monitoring Repurposing Evidence Test Package; purpose-creep and coercive-use red teams with linkability and appeal drills. | Threat Register T-032; Claims and Evidence Register; Pilot Evidence Roadmap; Hardening Queue; Capture Dashboard Specification. |
| Real-capacity measurement | Bind capacity rules to category-specific definitions, confidence bands, physical samples, methodology diversity, and dashboard comprehension. | Sphere; IPC; WHO SARA; WHO/UNICEF JMP; UN-Water; IEA; UN/OECD statistical-practice standards. | Capacity Measurement Evidence Test Package; field sampling and oracle-failure drill. | Threat Register T-006/T-020/T-021/T-024; Claims and Evidence Register; Pilot Evidence Roadmap. |
| Contribution model definition | Treat contribution as plural stewardship, not rank; test misuse by institutions outside the civic system. | OECD integrity sources; EU AI Act social-scoring risk; EEOC employment selection guidance; ILO forced-labour indicators; ICO workplace monitoring guidance. | Service Record Misuse Evidence Test Package; employer/vendor/school/housing/platform/lender/insurer misuse red team; contribution-category equity audit; burnout and participation analysis. | Threat Register T-004/T-008/T-009; Hardening Queue; Claims and Evidence Register; Pilot Evidence Roadmap. |
| Semantic bureaucracy risk | Audit both capture and over-freezing; require examples showing who benefits from every definitional change. | OECD integrity indicators on monitoring and implementation gaps. | Protected-term registry red-team and ordinary-reader review. | Threat Register T-007; Hardening Queue. |
| Subsidiarity aggregate drift | Track cumulative venue movement upward, not only individual decisions. | Ostrom commons evidence and IASC commons field orientation. | Annual decision-venue concentration report and remand-case audit. | Threat Register T-027; Hardening Queue; Pilot Evidence Roadmap. |
| Founding legitimacy | Define who may found, who may object, what consent threshold means, who qualifies as independent civil society, and how exit is rehearsed before dependency forms. | Ostrom local-governance evidence; OECD integrity evidence on institutional trust and implementation gaps. | Founding Legitimacy Dossier artifact-status register, Founding Consent and Civil-Society Review Evidence Test Package, public objection process, consent event evidence, 2/3 threshold certification, opt-out and aid-nonconditioning proof, exit rehearsal, founder conflict register, dignity-floor non-coercion review, reviewer challenge log, and independent review. | Threat Register T-017/T-022/T-026/T-033; Claims and Evidence Register; Pilot Evidence Roadmap; Public Readiness Guide. |
| CRP bootstrap chain | Resolve the governance chain deadlock: CRP required to confirm sub-Ombuds; full sortition-based CRP not yet constituted; Ombuds-dependent launch gates blocked. Design closure achieved (2026-05-16): provisional CRP mechanism adopted as bootstrap path. Evidence closure still requires completing the human input form, executing the draw, satisfying ANNEX_U Section U2 conditions, and running the confirmation votes. | ANNEX_N; ANNEX_U Section U2; ANNEX_AI Section 2.1; ANNEX_AH P-014 five-stage process. | Execute provisional CRP draw per docs/governance/Provisional_CRP_Draw_and_Roster_Certification_Packet.md; complete all ANNEX_U Section U2 pre-effect conditions; run sub-Ombuds confirmations; publish all required artifacts. | docs/governance/OQ_CRP_1_Adopted_Interpretation.md; docs/governance/Provisional_CRP_Owner_Decision_Record.md; docs/governance/Provisional_CRP_Draw_and_Roster_Certification_Packet.md; docs/governance/Federated_Ombuds_Constitution_Packet.md; docs/audits/remaining-blockers-agent-plan.md A1. |
| Institutional self-protection | Treat every review body as a capture surface; publish voting concentration, staffing overlap, and funding dependence. | OECD Public Integrity Indicators and Handbook. | Review-body self-protection red team and ordinary-reader authority tracing. | Threat Register T-005/T-008/T-016; Hardening Queue. |
| Evidence status drift | Prevent public language from moving faster than proof. | Evidence Ladder; Claims and Evidence Register; outside audit discipline. | Evidence ladder review before every ACTIVE, RESOLVED, or evidence-backed claim. | Claims and Evidence Register, Patch Log, public docs. |
| Parameter calibration drift | Bind high-risk FC values to evidence, revision triggers, and capture routes. | Parameter Calibration Register plus evidence packages for identity, capacity, Service Record, Commons Return, and Essential Access refusal. | Parameter calibration review before reserved or provisional FC values are bound. | founding/commitments.md, architecture/parameter_registry.md, SPECIFICATIONS, Acceptance Protocol. |
| Essential-sector transition without hostage power | Preserve business incentives for real production while removing scarcity, patent, grid, fuel, and procurement leverage over the survival floor. | Conglomerate Transition Dossier; Essential-Sector Refusal Test Package; IEA, EIA, FTC, CMS, OECD, WHO, ASHP, NREL, NARUC, FATF, and OpenSecrets/Visual Capitalist evidence anchors. | Sector-by-sector compliant-margin test, fallback public capacity, refusal survivability, non-compliant-country trade-membrane drill, medicine/PBM refusal drill, and grid/logistics delay drill. | Annex AT, Hardening Queue, Pilot Evidence Roadmap, Capture Dashboard Specification, Parameter Calibration Register. |

---

## Commons Return and Fiscal Sustainability Track

Commons Return and Universal Stake are now core architecture, not a side mechanism. They need a stricter evidence track than ordinary patch support because they carry the "who pays" question.

Before any public claim that the fiscal architecture works at scale, the project needs:

- a baseline Essential Access cost model by region and category;
- a hard-to-serve delivery cost model;
- a source-by-source Commons Return revenue model;
- a Universal Stake formula, reserve rule, and election-timing capture test;
- a Flow issuance and inflation/debasement tolerance;
- a public-rail operating cost model;
- an incidence model by wealth, income, household type, disability, age, region, renter/owner/stewardship status, and small-operator status;
- a legal-wrapper review covering trusts, trust protectors, family offices, foundations, donor-advised vehicles, beneficial ownership chains, platform spin-offs, license fragmentation, land-control contracts, enterprise-control proxies, external holding companies, side letters, and staged succession;
- procurement red-team scenarios for favoritism, collusion, tailored specifications, and post-award substitution;
- a remaining-tax necessity review naming which taxes, fees, or charges remain, why they remain, whether they are transitional or permanent, and how prohibited bases are screened;
- a public explanation showing how Commons Return differs from income tax, wealth tax, routine demurrage, political dividend, confiscation, or arbitrary account decay.

The correct current claim is: **Commons Return and Universal Stake are designed as a better-targeted public-return architecture than routine demurrage, but they still need fiscal modeling, distributional testing, privacy review, public comprehension testing, and legal-wrapper analysis before any evidence-backed claim.**

headings.text: Commons Return and Fiscal Sustainability Track

docs.title: Architecture Source Map
docs.summary: This document explains the first-principles architecture behind the Humane Constitution and names the outside sources that help pressure-test it.
docs.content:
# Architecture Source Map

This document explains the first-principles architecture behind the Humane Constitution and names the outside sources that help pressure-test it.

The architecture is an original synthesis. The sources below support parts of the diagnosis and design discipline; they do not prove that the full constitutional system works.

---

## Core Collapse States

The project is organized around preventing three structural collapse states.

| Collapse state | First-principles failure | Project response | Main evidence burden |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Survival-Trade Bind** | A person must succeed in markets, employment, credit, or exchange to stay alive, safe, housed, treated, or civically legible. | Essential Access, Constitutional Survival Minimum, identity-continuity rules, non-convertibility, and capacity measurement. | Identity recovery, Essential Access delivery, shadow-convertibility testing, real-capacity measurement, appeal continuity, and scarcity drills. |
| **Power-Wealth Convergence** | Accumulated wealth becomes formal or practical authority over rules, enforcement, public measurement, civic access, or institutional design. | Flow / Voice / Service Record separation, anti-conversion rules, public records, beneficial-control review, anti-rent controls, and independent oversight. | Proxy-market tests, institutional-capture review, public-banking simulation, anti-corruption audit, legal-wrapper review, and FAP capture testing. |
| **Static-Advantage Loop** | Early-entry advantage, inheritance, expertise, procedural control, or gatekeeping hardens into permanent class position. | Commons Return, bounded capital claims, decay where appropriate, rotation, anti-dynasty rules, new-participant access, subsidiarity review, exit protections, and bottleneck audits. | Service Record misuse testing, concentration dashboards, legal-wrapper review, founding-legitimacy tests, and governance red-team exercises. |

These collapse states are a diagnostic lens, not a status label. A mechanism can be written, proposed, active, or evidence-backed only under the status language in the [Claims and Evidence Register](./Claims_Evidence_Register.md).

For the threat-by-threat mapping, use the [Collapse-State Crosswalk](./Collapse_State_Crosswalk.md). For the test ladder that decides when a collapse-state claim may be strengthened, use the [Evidence Ladder](./Evidence_Ladder.md).

---

## Instrument Map

The project does not use one universal instrument that can buy everything.

| Function | Existing project instrument | What it must not become |
|---|---|---|
| Basic survival and functional continuity | Essential Access / Constitutional Survival Minimum | A commodity, debt instrument, punishment tool, patronage channel, or gatekeeping reward. |
| Optional exchange and enterprise | Flow | A route to civic authority, survival priority, enforcement privilege, or permanent bottleneck control. |
| Public participation and correction | Voice | A purchased vote, transferable influence asset, status market, or wealth-weighted rule channel. |
| Contribution recognition | Service Record | A total human-worth score, employability rank, caste marker, or permanent civic aristocracy. |
| Scarcity response | Shared Storehouse | Ordinary trade inventory, permanent emergency administration, or discretionary rationing power. |

The core safety rule is:

> No stored advantage may purchase access to survival, rule-authority, or permanent dominance.

The stronger operational version is:

> Trade wealth must not directly or indirectly produce durable rule advantage, enforcement privilege, survival priority, or control over essential bottlenecks.

This broader version matters because corruption usually travels through indirect routes: employers, landlords, contractors, shell entities, professional networks, media influence, standards bodies, family offices, foundations, and dependency relationships.

---

## Source Map

| Design claim | Outside source anchor | What it supports | What it does not prove | Evidence level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Survival should not depend only on market success. | [Universal Basic Services: Theory and Practice](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/10080177/) | Essential services can be framed as public access infrastructure that supports welfare, safety, opportunity, and democratic participation. | It does not prove this project's Essential Access, CSM, identity, logistics, or funding mechanics. | Level 3 — Internal consistency review |
| Shared-resource systems need boundaries, monitoring, sanctions, conflict resolution, local rule participation, and nested governance. | [Elinor Ostrom Nobel Prize Lecture](https://www.nobelprize.org/uploads/2018/06/ostrom_lecture.pdf) | Durable commons governance depends on concrete institutional design, not declarations of shared purpose. | It does not prove a federation-scale constitutional economy can inherit commons-governance success. | Level 3 — Internal consistency review |
| Wealth can distort rule outcomes. | [Gilens and Page, Testing Theories of American Politics](https://doi.org/10.1017/S1537592714001595) | Economic elites and organized business interests can have substantial independent policy influence in the U.S. cases they modeled. | It is U.S.-specific, debated, and does not prove this project's anti-conversion mechanisms work. | Level 3 — Internal consistency review |
| Accumulated advantage can compound over time. | [Thomas Piketty, Capital in the Twenty-First Century](https://www.hup.harvard.edu/books/9780674430006) | Long-run accumulation and inherited advantage can produce concentration dynamics that require structural attention. | It does not prove Commons Return, bounded capital claims, decay mechanisms, or anti-rent rules will be fair, effective, or politically legitimate. | Level 3 — Internal consistency review |
| Non-convertibility is the core safety hypothesis. | Synthesis from the above sources plus the project's threat model. | The sources support the diagnosis that survival-market dependence, wealth-rule influence, commons capture, and compounding advantage are real design pressures. | No source proves perfect non-convertibility. Shadow markets, proxies, legal wrappers, and informal patronage remain permanent attack surfaces. | Level 3 — Internal consistency review |
| Tier 1 drift should be detectable, not trusted by assertion. | [NIST FIPS 180-4](https://www.nist.gov/publications/secure-hash-standard), [RFC 6962](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6962), [NISTIR 8214](https://www.nist.gov/publications/threshold-schemes-cryptographic-primitives), [NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/57/pt1/r5/final), and [OpenZeppelin TimelockController](https://docs.openzeppelin.com/contracts/4.x/api/governance) | Hashes, append-only logs, threshold signing, key management, and timelocks are real integrity patterns. | They do not prove this project's operators, keyholders, software supply chain, founding authority, or public response window will work. | Level 4 — Adversarial paper test |

---

> **Current architecture status: Level 3 (Designed mechanism).** The project may not claim Level 5 or above for any design claim until pilot evidence exists. The Tier 1 tamper-evidence layer is at Level 4 based on the proven cryptographic standards it uses, not on this project's implementation having been tested.

---

## Claim Boundaries

The project may honestly claim that it contains a serious channel-separation architecture designed to resist three collapse states.

It should not claim that:

- survival access has been proven deliverable at scale;
- wealth-to-rule conversion has been eliminated;
- static advantage can be fully prevented;
- Commons Return, Universal Stake, or dormant demurrage backstops have been proven fair or effective;
- Service Record cannot become social ranking;
- measurement oracles can perfectly reflect physical reality;
- emergency powers cannot normalize;
- founding legitimacy has already been earned.
- Tier 1 implementation drift is impossible merely because the documents contain hashes, signatures, and timelocks.

The accurate current claim is narrower: the document set defines mechanisms, threat models, patch histories, evidence needs, and pilot gates that make those collapse states visible and testable.

---

## Review Questions

Use these questions when reviewing any threat, patch, annex, pilot, or public claim:

1. Does this mechanism reduce one collapse state while creating another?
2. Can a wealthy actor route around the formal rule through proxies, contractors, family networks, media influence, shell entities, or professional control?
3. Does the Life Channel depend on administrators whose discretion could become a survival gate?
4. Can Service Record become rank by effect even if the text says it is not a score?
5. Are real capacity, delivery, and appeal outcomes measured physically enough to catch paper compliance?
6. Does emergency authority automatically sunset, and can the public understand the sunset evidence?
7. Can new participants enter without being permanently subordinate to early entrants?
8. What evidence would falsify the claim that this mechanism resists the collapse state it targets?

---

## Recent Annex Additions

This section records significant structural additions made to individual annexes after the initial architecture was established. For full threat-to-annex linkage see `ANNEX_AH.md § AH8` and `docs/governance/Provenance_Map.md`.

### ANNEX_AM §AM8 — Constitutional Integrity Panel (CIP)

Added by P-051. Addresses T-022 open problem: constitutional body now specified.

§AM8 — Constitutional Integrity Panel (CIP): 7-member independent body with staggered terms, 0.01%-of-Flow-issuance constitutionally fixed funding, 5-of-7 quorum for Tier 1 ratification, automatic hollowing triggers (90-day vacancy, 30-day post-mortem lapse). Added by P-051. Addresses T-022 open problem: constitutional body now specified.

### ANNEX_AT §AT6.6 — Compliant Alternative Supplier Pre-Registration (CASP)

Added by P-050. CASP is primary T-028 mitigation; public receiver authority is fallback.

§AT6.6 — CASP: mandatory pre-registration of backup suppliers with automatic-activation contracts (9 chokepoints specified, gap-window calculation required). Added by P-050. CASP is primary T-028 mitigation; public receiver authority is fallback.

### ANNEX_AI §4.12 — Deliberate-Manufacture Standard

Added by P-052. Closes T-019 open problem.

§4.12 — Deliberate-manufacture standard: 4-criterion assessment (timing, proportionality, prior-basis, knowledge), 24-hour Plenum window, asymmetric default favoring PCRP. Added by P-052. Closes T-019 open problem.

---

## Founding Commitments Status

FC-140 through FC-145 (AED fraud/exclusion bands) now bound. FC-052, FC-053, FC-054, FC-056, FC-072, FC-148 through FC-150 now have proposed values pending founding deliberation.

docs.title: External Evidence Register
docs.summary: This register records outside sources that can pressure-test the Humane Constitution against real-world practice.
docs.content:
# External Evidence Register

This register records outside sources that can pressure-test the Humane Constitution against real-world practice.

External evidence does not prove that this constitutional design works. It shows that a risk, mechanism, standard, failure pattern, or institutional constraint exists outside the project. Every source should therefore be used with two questions: what does it support, and what does it not prove?

See also: [Architecture Source Map](./Architecture_Source_Map.md), [Open Problems Resolution Docket](./Open_Problems_Resolution_Docket.md), [Threat Resolution Matrix](./Threat_Resolution_Matrix.md), [Claims and Evidence Register](./Claims_Evidence_Register.md), [Conglomerate Transition Dossier](./Conglomerate_Transition_Dossier.md), and [Pilot Evidence Roadmap](./Pilot_Evidence_Roadmap.md).

---

## Source Discipline

- Prefer official standards, peer-reviewed research, public audit material, government reports, central-bank research, and established humanitarian standards.
- Do not treat a source as proof of the whole constitutional system.
- Do not cite a source merely because it agrees with the project. Prefer sources that expose constraints, implementation gaps, and failure modes.
- Every source should be tied to at least one threat, open problem, pilot test, or residual-risk statement.

---

## Evidence Sources

| Source | Type | Relevant problems | What it supports | What it does not prove |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| [Universal Basic Services: Theory and Practice](https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/10080177/) | Public policy research report | Survival-Trade Bind, Essential Access, public service access | Essential services can be treated as public access infrastructure that supports welfare, safety, opportunity, and democratic participation. | It does not prove this project's Essential Access, CSM, identity, logistics, or funding design. |
| [Elinor Ostrom Nobel Prize Lecture](https://www.nobelprize.org/uploads/2018/06/ostrom_lecture.pdf) | Nobel lecture / commons-governance research | Static-Advantage Loop, subsidiarity, monitoring, sanctions, conflict resolution | Durable shared-resource systems need clear boundaries, monitoring, graduated sanctions, conflict-resolution mechanisms, local participation, and nested institutions. | It does not prove that commons design scales cleanly into a federation-wide constitutional economy. |
| [Gilens and Page, Testing Theories of American Politics](https://doi.org/10.1017/S1537592714001595) | Peer-reviewed political science | Power-Wealth Convergence, wealth-to-rule capture, proxy influence | Economic elites and organized business interests can have substantial independent influence over policy outcomes in the U.S. cases modeled. | It is U.S.-specific and does not prove this project's Voice/Flow separation blocks practical influence. |
| [Thomas Piketty, Capital in the Twenty-First Century](https://www.hup.harvard.edu/books/9780674430006) | Economic history / inequality research | Static-Advantage Loop, accumulated advantage, anti-rent pressure | Long-run accumulation and inherited advantage can produce concentration dynamics that require structural attention. | It does not prove demurrage, decay, or anti-rent controls are fair, effective, or legitimate. |
| [NIST SP 800-63-4 Digital Identity Guidelines](https://pages.nist.gov/800-63-4/) | Official technical standard | T-002, identity-stack fraud calibration | Digital identity needs risk management, assurance levels, privacy, fraud controls, customer-experience evaluation, and continuous evaluation. | It does not prove a survival-floor identity system can be both fraud-resistant and non-exclusionary. |
| [World Bank ID4D](https://id4d.worldbank.org/about-us), [ID4D Practitioner's Guide](https://id4d.worldbank.org/guide), and [Principles on Identification for Sustainable Development](https://id4d.worldbank.org/guide/1-principles) | Development policy / implementation guidance | T-002, founding legitimacy, access continuity | Identification systems must be inclusive, trusted, rights-supporting, privacy-protective, accountable, and accessible to enable access to services. | It does not provide a complete fraud/exclusion calibration for this project. |
| [World Bank ID4D exclusion-risk guidance](https://id4d.worldbank.org/guide/assess-risks) | Development implementation guidance | T-002, identity recovery, vulnerable-population access | ID systems can exclude poor, rural, elderly, disabled, displaced, refugee, stateless, digitally fragile, and other vulnerable groups if barriers are not actively assessed and mitigated. | It does not identify the local exclusion rate or acceptable threshold for this project. |
| [UNHCR registration and identity management guidance](https://www.unhcr.org/registration-guidance/chapter3/access-to-registration/) | Refugee protection / registration guidance | T-002, displaced-person registration, non-arbitrary access | Registration for displaced people must be accessible, non-arbitrary, and designed around practical barriers. | Refugee registration guidance does not solve ordinary civic identity, fraud, or long-term recovery. |
| [W3C Verifiable Credentials Data Model v2.0](https://www.w3.org/news/2025/the-verifiable-credentials-2-0-family-of-specifications-is-now-a-w3c-recommendation/) | Web credential standard | T-002, privacy-preserving credentials, selective disclosure | Cryptographically verifiable, privacy-respecting credentials are a mature technical design space. | Verifiable credentials do not by themselves solve enrollment, coercion, device loss, issuer capture, or exclusion. |
| [Sphere Handbook](https://spherestandards.org/handbook/) | Humanitarian field standard | T-006, T-024, Essential Access, CSM evidence | Humanitarian response uses minimum standards for WASH, food security, shelter, and health, giving real-world anchors for survival-floor testing. | Emergency humanitarian standards are not a full constitutional economy or ordinary-life dignity basket. |
| [IPC Manual 3.1](https://www.ipcinfo.org/ipcinfo-website/resources/ipc-manual/en/) | Food-security classification standard | T-006, T-020, food capacity and scarcity measurement | Food insecurity classification uses evidence convergence, international standards, and severity thresholds for high-consequence food-security decisions. | It does not measure every essential category or solve supplier manipulation. |
| [WHO Service Availability and Readiness Assessment](https://www.who.int/data/data-collection-tools/service-availability-and-readiness-assessment-%28sara%29?ua=1) | Health facility measurement tool | T-006, health/care capacity measurement | Health service capacity requires facility lists, surveys, tracer indicators, equipment, medicines, staffing, and readiness measures. | It does not prove actual real-time care availability or service quality under crisis. |
| [WHO/UNICEF WASH monitoring](https://www.who.int/activities/monitoring-water-sanitation-and-hygiene) and [UN-Water SDG 6 monitoring](https://www.unwater.org/publications/sdg-6-monitoring) | Water and sanitation monitoring guidance | T-006, water capacity and WASH measurement | WASH monitoring depends on comparable indicators, system monitoring, availability, safety, and explicit definitions. | Global monitoring cadence is not fast enough for emergency rationing decisions. |
| [IEA energy data and statistics](https://www.iea.org/data-and-statistics/about) | Energy data practice | T-006, energy capacity measurement | Energy security policy depends on consistent, accurate, timely supply and demand data, including near-term data collections. | It does not prove local Essential Access energy availability. |
| [UN Fundamental Principles of Official Statistics](https://unstats.un.org/fpos) and [OECD Good Statistical Practice](https://www.oecd.org/en/toolkits/good-statistical-practice.html) | Statistical integrity standards | T-006, T-020, public measurement legitimacy | Public statistics require integrity, professional independence, transparency, equal access, quality, and accountability. | Statistical principles do not prevent political, supplier, or standards-body capture by themselves. |
| [OECD AI Principles](https://www.oecd.org/en/topics/ai-principles.html) | AI governance standard | T-021, algorithmic oracle capture | AI systems need transparency, robustness, safety, accountability, and lifecycle risk management. | AI principles do not prove algorithmic oracle independence or correctness. |
| [EU AI Act Article 5 and Recital 31](https://ai-act-service-desk.ec.europa.eu/en/ai-act/article-5) | Statutory AI-risk framework | T-004, T-008, Service Record misuse, social-credit risk | Social scoring can produce detrimental or disproportionate treatment in contexts unrelated to where the data was generated. | The AI Act does not validate this project's non-AI civic ledger design or solve non-algorithmic reputation misuse. |
| [EEOC employment tests and selection procedures guidance](https://www.eeoc.gov/laws/guidance/employment-tests-and-selection-procedures) and [EEOC AI employment materials](https://www.eeoc.gov/eeoc-publications) | Employment civil-rights guidance | T-004, Service Record misuse, employability scoring | Selection procedures and automated employment tools can create disparate impact and require validation, job-relatedness, and less-discriminatory alternatives. | U.S. employment law does not cover every housing, vendor, civic, or social-status misuse route. |
| [ILO indicators of forced labour](https://www.ilo.org/publications/ilo-indicators-forced-labour) | Labour-rights operational guidance | T-004, contribution coercion, Survival-Trade Bind | Coercion can arise through vulnerability, threats, deception, and dependence; contribution systems must not make survival, identity, or civic standing effectively compulsory work. | Forced-labour indicators are detection aids, not a complete contribution-incentive architecture. |
| [ICO workplace monitoring guidance](https://ico.org.uk/about-the-ico/media-centre/news-and-blogs/2023/10/ico-publishes-guidance-to-ensure-lawful-monitoring-in-the-workplace/) | Data-protection / workplace monitoring guidance | T-004, contribution verification, worker surveillance | Workplace monitoring creates privacy and fairness risks and should be lawful, transparent, and proportionate. | UK data-protection guidance does not define this project's constitutional boundary for Service Record. |
| [OECD Public Integrity Indicators](https://www.oecd-ilibrary.org/en/topics/public-integrity.html) | Public integrity data framework | T-005, T-008, T-016, T-022, institutional self-protection | Formal anti-corruption rules can differ from implementation; integrity needs data, monitoring, accountability, sanctions, and institutional capacity. | It does not prove this project's oversight bodies will resist capture. |
| [OECD Public Integrity Handbook](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/2020/05/oecd-public-integrity-handbook_598692a5.html) | Public governance guidance | T-005, T-008, T-016, T-022 | Integrity systems need coherent strategy, accountability, culture, risk management, and enforcement. | It does not remove the need for local audits and adversarial testing. |
| [OECD integrity in public procurement](https://www.oecd.org/en/topics/sub-issues/integrity-in-public-procurement.html) | Procurement integrity guidance | T-025, Commons Return implementation, contract-commitment architecture | Procurement and infrastructure spending are high-corruption-risk areas, especially when large sums, complex supply chains, discretion, and urgency combine. | It does not validate the specific Commons Return, Universal Stake, Flow, or contract-commitment design. |
| [OECD public procurement](https://www.oecd.org/en/topics/public-procurement.html) | Public procurement data / governance guidance | Essential-sector conglomerates, procurement capture, public fallback capacity | Public procurement is large enough to shape markets: OECD public procurement was 12.9% of GDP in 2021, with 61.2% of procurement spending at sub-national level. | Procurement scale does not prove that public purchasing can discipline oil, energy, medicine, or logistics conglomerates without capture. |
| [FATF beneficial ownership guidance](https://www.fatf-gafi.org/en/publications/Fatfrecommendations/Transparency-and-beneficial-ownership.html) | International anti-money-laundering guidance | T-001, T-025, anti-rent legal wrappers | Corporate vehicles, trusts, and legal arrangements can hide true control; beneficial ownership transparency is necessary for enforcement. | It does not prove that beneficial ownership rules can catch every lawful elite workaround. |
| [FATF Guidance on Beneficial Ownership of Legal Persons](https://www.fatf-gafi.org/content/fatf-gafi/en/publications/Fatfrecommendations/Guidance-Beneficial-Ownership-Legal-Persons.html) | International anti-money-laundering guidance | T-025, anti-dynasty ownership, enterprise-control workarounds | A multi-pronged approach to beneficial ownership information is more effective than a single source of information. | It does not settle constitutional limits on property, stewardship, or succession. |
| [IEA World Energy Investment 2024](https://www.iea.org/reports/world-energy-investment-2024/overview-and-key-findings) | Energy investment benchmark | External dependency capture, fossil transition, strategic floors | Energy transition occurs inside a system with very large fossil and clean-energy capital flows: 2024 global energy investment exceeded USD 3 trillion, while upstream oil and gas investment was about USD 570 billion. | It does not prove that the protocol can redirect incumbent energy firms or replace them if they refuse. |
| [EIA petroleum import/export FAQ](https://www.eia.gov/tools/faqs/faq.php?id=727&t=6) | Government energy data | External dependency capture, import leverage, Annex AT | Even net petroleum exporters can rely on gross imports and crude-specific supply chains; U.S. 2023 petroleum imports were about 8.51 million barrels/day and crude imports about 6.48 million barrels/day. | U.S. data does not define the dependency profile of a future protocol jurisdiction. |
| [EIA electricity generation](https://www.eia.gov/energyexplained/electricity/electricity-in-the-us-generation-capacity-and-sales.php) | Government electricity data | Energy transition, natural-monopoly utility governance | In 2023, about 60% of U.S. utility-scale electricity came from fossil fuels and 43.1% from natural gas, showing why abrupt exclusion of fossil-linked operators can threaten continuity. | It does not justify continued fossil dependence or prove any utility-transition design. |
| [IEA World Energy Outlook 2024](https://www.iea.org/reports/world-energy-outlook-2024/overview-and-key-findings) | Energy transition scenario analysis | Fossil transition, stranded-asset exposure, energy-security timing | IEA scenarios show clean-energy growth, electrification, and efficiency can change fossil-demand trajectories; in STEPS, demand for all three fossil fuels peaks before 2030. | It does not prove any specific fossil asset will strand, or that a protocol jurisdiction can manage transition costs, jobs, reliability, or political resistance. |
| [Carbon Tracker: Unburnable Carbon Ten Years On](https://carbontracker.org/reports/unburnable-carbon-ten-years-on/) | Climate-finance / stranded-asset analysis | Fossil transition, stranded assets, incumbent balance-sheet risk | Carbon Tracker estimates that a large share of listed fossil-fuel reserves would be unburnable in a 1.5°C-aligned pathway and identifies material oil-and-gas asset-stranding exposure. | It is scenario-based financial-risk analysis, not a valuation of every firm or proof that stranded-asset losses will occur on a particular schedule. |
| [NREL: Major Drivers of Long-Term Distribution Transformer Demand](https://research-hub.nrel.gov/en/publications/major-drivers-of-long-term-distribution-transformer-demand) | National laboratory grid supply-chain report | Grid capacity, transformer bottlenecks, electrification constraints | NREL reports major U.S. distribution-transformer shortages, lead times up to 2 years, large recent price increases, and constraints from labor, materials, and component supply chains. | It does not prove the protocol can procure transformers, accelerate manufacturing, or avoid local utility interconnection delays. |
| [NARUC Commissioners' Desk Reference: History of Utility Regulation](https://www.naruc.org/commissioners-desk-reference-manual/1-history-of-utility-regulation-and-regulatory-agencies/) | Public utility regulation reference | Public utility regulation, natural-monopoly governance, fair-rate oversight | Utility regulation commonly grants monopoly franchises while state commissions approve rates and services, aiming for safe, reliable service at just and reasonable rates while balancing ratepayer and utility financing needs. | It does not prove that regulated monopoly is the only model, that regulators avoid capture, or that this project's utility-governance design is adequate. |
| [FTC interim staff report on prescription drug middlemen](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/07/ftc-releases-interim-staff-report-prescription-drug-middlemen) | Government competition / market-structure report | Medicine access, PBM capture, vertical integration | The top three PBMs processed nearly 80% of about 6.6 billion U.S. pharmacy prescriptions in 2023, and affiliated pharmacies of the three largest PBMs accounted for nearly 70% of specialty drug revenue. | PBM concentration does not prove the same structure would exist in a protocol jurisdiction, but it shows the access-control pattern is real. |
| [CMS/AHA summary of 2023 national health spending](https://www.aha.org/news/headline/2024-12-19-cms-national-health-spending-increased-75-2023) | Health expenditure data summary | Medicine procurement, health-sector leverage | U.S. national health spending reached about USD 4.9 trillion in 2023 and prescription drug spending increased 11.4%, showing the scale of incumbent economic stakes. | Spending scale does not prove that price controls, public procurement, or medicine fallback capacity will work. |
| [Visual Capitalist summary using OpenSecrets data](https://www.visualcapitalist.com/ranked-which-u-s-industries-spend-the-most-on-lobbying/) | Lobbying expenditure summary | Conglomerate rule-shaping, capture dashboard metrics | In 2023, U.S. federal lobbying reached about USD 4.3 billion; pharmaceuticals/health products spent about USD 382.6 million, oil and gas about USD 133.0 million, and electric utilities about USD 128.5 million. | Lobbying spend is not the whole capture channel and U.S. federal lobbying data omits many informal or non-U.S. influence routes. |
| [WHO access to medicines and health products](https://www.who.int/our-work/access-to-medicines-and-health-products) | Global health policy | Essential medicines, medicine fallback capacity | WHO identifies medicine affordability, shortages, stockouts, and substandard/falsified products as obstacles to universal health coverage. | It does not validate the protocol's CSM medicine list, stockpile duration, or licensing policy. |
| [FTC Orange Book patent-listing challenges](https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2024/04/ftc-expands-patent-listing-challenges-targeting-more-300-junk-listings-diabetes-weight-loss-asthma) | Government competition enforcement | Pharma patents, evergreening risk, generic-entry delay | FTC challenged more than 300 Orange Book patent listings across 20 brand-name products in 2024, stating that improper listings can delay cheaper generic alternatives and keep brand-name prices high. | It does not prove every challenged patent is invalid, that all evergreening claims are unlawful, or that protocol licensing rules would survive legal and supply-chain constraints. |
| [ASHP drug shortage statistics](https://www.ashp.org/drug-shortages/shortage-resources/drug-shortages-statistics) | Health-system shortage tracker | Medicine shortages, essential-medicine resilience, pharmacy operations | ASHP reported 223 active U.S. drug shortages as of March 2026 and notes that a single shortage can affect many patients while adding operational burden for health systems. | It does not identify the exact causes of every shortage or prove that stockpiles, procurement reform, or public manufacturing would prevent shortages. |
| [NIST FIPS 180-4 Secure Hash Standard](https://www.nist.gov/publications/secure-hash-standard) | Cryptographic standard | Implementation drift, Tier 1 tamper evidence, architecture layer | Hash algorithms can generate digests that detect whether messages have changed since the digest was generated. | Hashes do not prove political legitimacy, correct implementation, or lawful amendment safety. |
| [RFC 6962 Certificate Transparency](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6962) | Internet integrity protocol | Drift chain, append-only logs, public consistency checks | Append-only Merkle logs, audit paths, consistency proofs, and signatures are real patterns for public detectability. | Certificate Transparency is not this project's drift chain and does not prove monitors will check it. |
| [NISTIR 8214 Threshold Schemes for Cryptographic Primitives](https://www.nist.gov/publications/threshold-schemes-cryptographic-primitives) | Cryptography guidance | Tier 1 keyholder threshold, amendment capture | Threshold schemes can distribute cryptographic operations so compromise below a threshold need not compromise the whole. | Threshold schemes do not solve holder selection, coercion, bribery, or social capture. |
| [NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/57/pt1/r5/final) | Key-management guidance | Keyholder custody, rotation, recovery, implementation drift | Key management needs lifecycle controls, metadata protection, access control, inventory, and operational discipline. | It does not certify this project's key custody, holder succession, or recovery process. |
| [OpenZeppelin TimelockController documentation](https://docs.openzeppelin.com/contracts/4.x/api/governance) | Practitioner governance pattern | Tier 1 timelock, public response window | Timelocks are a known pattern for delaying privileged changes and giving users time to respond or exit. | Smart-contract timelocks do not prove constitutional legitimacy or the correct duration for this project. |
| [NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0](https://www.nist.gov/cyberframework) | Official cyber-risk framework | T-030, cyber availability, incident response, recovery governance | Cyber risk management requires governance, identification, protection, detection, response, and recovery functions rather than only prevention. | It does not prove this protocol can keep Essential Access reachable during an outage. |
| [NIST SP 800-34 Rev. 1 Contingency Planning Guide](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/34/r1/upd1/final) | Official contingency-planning guidance | T-030, outage continuity, alternate processing, backup and recovery | Information-system continuity requires planned, tested recovery procedures, alternate processing, and operational contingency planning. | It does not define this project's survival-floor manual fallback or acceptable outage tolerance. |
| [CISA StopRansomware Guide](https://www.cisa.gov/stopransomware/ransomware-guide) | Government ransomware guidance | T-030, ransomware continuity, offline recovery | Ransomware readiness requires prevention, response, backup, recovery, and operational discipline, including recovery planning before an incident. | It does not prove backups preserve food, medicine, shelter, or provider settlement continuity. |
| [IMF Working Paper: Monetary Policy with Negative Interest Rates](https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/WP/Issues/2018/08/27/Monetary-Policy-with-Negative-Interest-Rates-Decoupling-Cash-from-Electronic-Money-46076) | Central-bank / monetary-policy research | Superseded demurrage design context; dormant backstop caution | Negative yields on money-like instruments are technically and institutionally studied; design details and cash/electronic convertibility matter. | Historical/cautionary only after P-066; it does not validate Commons Return, Universal Stake, or any revived dormant backstop. |
| [Buiter and Panigirtzoglou, Economic Journal: Gesell's Solution](https://academic.oup.com/ej/article-abstract/113/490/723/5079623) | Peer-reviewed monetary economics | T-025, demurrage theory | Demurrage-like negative interest on currency has a recognized theoretical lineage and formal macroeconomic treatment. | It does not show that demurrage is socially legitimate, administratively fair, or capture-resistant. |
| [Federal Reserve FEDS: On the Negatives of Negative Interest Rates](https://www.federalreserve.gov/econres/feds/on-the-negatives-of-negative-interest-rates.htm) | Central-bank research | T-025, demurrage risk, banking and investment side effects | Negative-rate regimes can distort investment decisions, welfare, output, bank profitability, and deposit transmission depending on design. | It does not reject all demurrage designs; it warns that negative-rate instruments have distributional and institutional side effects. |
| [NBER: A Simple Model of a Central Bank Digital Currency](https://www.nber.org/papers/w31198) | Economic research | T-025, public monetary rails, demurrage-capable digital money | CBDC models study tradeoffs among cash, digital money, rates of return, tax evasion, and policy tools. | It does not prove a public-money rail should use demurrage or that it would be politically acceptable. |
| [Godschalk, Does Demurrage Matter for Complementary Currencies?](https://www.ijccr.net/article/view/9173) | Complementary-currency research | Superseded demurrage evidence limits; local-currency analogies | The demurrage evidence base is context-sensitive; local and emergency currency experience should be treated as limited evidence, not proof of constitutional-scale monetary design. | Historical/cautionary only after P-066; it does not validate Commons Return, Universal Stake, public-rail funding, or anti-rent enforcement at scale. |
| [Elinor Ostrom Nobel Prize profile](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/economic-sciences/2009/ostrom/facts/) | Nobel Foundation summary of empirical research | T-027, subsidiarity, local consent | Long-lived commons can be governed by local rules and institutions rather than only markets or central states. | It does not automatically scale local commons principles to a federation-wide constitutional system. |
| [International Association for the Study of the Commons](https://iasc-commons.org/about-commons/) | Research association / field orientation | T-027, subsidiarity, local governance | Commons governance applies across natural, cultural, digital, urban, health, and other shared-resource domains. | It does not prescribe a single institutional design for this project. |
| [USDA Food and Nutrition Service, SNAP Quality Control Annual Reports](https://www.fns.usda.gov/snap/quality-control) | Government program quality-control data | FC-140, AED fraud and exclusion calibration | SNAP is the closest operational analogue to Essential Access — a large-scale government program providing survival-floor food access. SNAP error rates (payment error rate historically 2–3%) provide a real-world baseline for what fraud/error rates are achievable in a well-administered survival-floor program. The FC-140 targets (≤2% fraud, ≤5% upper bound) are calibrated against this baseline. | SNAP measures payment error (over- and under-payments), not identity fraud specifically. SNAP operates in a mature administrative context with established verification infrastructure. A new system would likely have higher initial error rates. |
| [UNHCR, Biometric Identity Management in Refugee Registration: Technical Documentation and Field Reports](https://www.unhcr.org/innovation/biometric-identity-management/) | Humanitarian field implementation documentation | FC-143 (displaced persons exclusion ≤6%), FC-144 (undocumented persons exclusion ≤8%) | UNHCR biometric registration programs are the primary large-scale precedent for identity verification in displaced and undocumented populations. UNHCR monitoring targets exclusion rates below 5% for registered refugees; the slightly looser bounds in FC-143/144 reflect the additional challenges of non-biometric and multi-modal identity systems. | UNHCR programs operate in emergency humanitarian contexts with different institutional authority than a constitutional system. Exclusion rates in stable contexts with appeals processes should be lower, not higher. |
| [Help America Vote Act (HAVA) implementation reports, U.S. Election Assistance Commission](https://www.eac.gov/election-officials/hava-funds) | Government election-administration reports | FC-141 Voice fraud target (≤3%) — calibration reference only | HAVA established federal standards for voter registration accuracy and verification. Voter registration error rates (ineligible registrations, duplicate registrations) provide a reference for what fraud/error rates are achievable in a civic participation verification context. | Voter registration fraud is extremely rare (<0.01% in most studies); the FC-141 target of ≤3% is far looser than voting fraud rates because Voice involves ongoing activity rather than a one-time registration event, and the consequences of wrongful exclusion are less severe than disenfranchisement. |
| [Government Accountability Office (GAO), Identity Verification: Agencies Should Improve Oversight of Third-Party Services](https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-22-105128) | Government accountability / audit report | FC-142 Service Record fraud target (≤5%) — identity verification context | GAO studies of federal identity verification programs show false acceptance rates of 2–5% in programs without biometric verification. FC-142's ≤5% target reflects that Service Record fraud is less consequential than Essential Access fraud and the system can tolerate a looser bound. | GAO reports cover legacy government systems; newer biometric and multi-factor systems achieve lower error rates. The FC-142 bound should be tightened as verification technology matures. |

---

## Source Gaps

The project still needs stronger outside evidence for:

- contribution incentives that do not become social ranking in live institutions,
- Commons Return, Universal Stake, and any dormant demurrage-backstop distributional effects by household wealth and business type,
- legal enforceability of zero-exemption project escrow across nested subcontractors,
- essential-sector conglomerate refusal, litigation, lobbying, and retaliation scenarios, especially drill results using the Essential-Sector Refusal Test Package rather than only external analogies,
- cyber availability and offline continuity under ransomware, breached keys, regional outage, provider settlement failure, and supply-chain compromise,
- identity recovery for undocumented, displaced, disabled, elderly, and digitally fragile people,
- last-resort no-credential survival access for people who cannot enroll, hold a wallet, keep a digital credential, safely disclose identity, or use ordinary digital interfaces,
- monitoring purpose limitation and anti-repurposing evidence for enforcement, dashboards, identity, delivery, provider, oracle, cyber, and anti-fraud data streams,
- transition legitimacy and consent thresholds, including real public notice, deliberation, consent-event, exit-rehearsal, and independent-review artifacts,
- practical anti-centralization audits for subsidiarity drift.

These gaps should remain visible until matched to specific studies, pilots, legal memoranda, or adversarial simulations.

docs.title: Implementation Drift Audit Package
docs.summary: This package defines how the project should test whether its hard locks, drift chain, parameter registry, amendment protocol, and implementation binding actually prevent quiet constitutional drift.
docs.content:
# Implementation Drift Audit Package

This package defines how the project should test whether its hard locks, drift chain, parameter registry, amendment protocol, and implementation binding actually prevent quiet constitutional drift.

It is not a claim that cryptography makes the system incorruptible. It is an audit package for proving, or falsifying, whether unauthorized drift is detectable, public, and correctable before it becomes normal operation.

---

## Honest Claim Boundary

The current honest claim is:

> The project contains a designed tamper-evidence layer for Tier 1 rules: parameter hashes, a drift chain, threshold signatures, a timelock, and startup verification by bound components.

The project should not yet claim:

- Tier 1 invariants are cryptographically guaranteed in practice;
- silent drift is impossible;
- the 7-of-9 holder set cannot be captured, coerced, bribed, or socially coordinated;
- software supply-chain compromise is solved;
- operators will always run compliant implementations;
- a lawful hostile amendment cannot pass through the valid process.

The design is credible only if independent reviewers can reproduce the hashes, verify the publication channels, test startup refusal, audit key custody, and see a public response when drift is detected.

---

## External Evidence Base

| Source | What it contributes | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| [NIST FIPS 180-4 Secure Hash Standard](https://www.nist.gov/publications/secure-hash-standard) | Secure hash functions can generate message digests that detect whether content changed after the digest was generated. | Hashes detect change; they do not decide whether a change is legitimate, safe, or politically captured. |
| [RFC 6962 Certificate Transparency](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6962) | Append-only Merkle logs, audit paths, consistency proofs, and signed public log structures are real integrity patterns. | Certificate Transparency is not this project's drift chain and does not prove monitor participation. |
| [NISTIR 8214 Threshold Schemes for Cryptographic Primitives](https://www.nist.gov/publications/threshold-schemes-cryptographic-primitives) | Threshold schemes distribute cryptographic operations so compromise of fewer than a threshold number of components need not compromise the whole. | Threshold cryptography does not solve holder selection, social coercion, bribery, or governance legitimacy. |
| [NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/sp/800/57/pt1/r5/final) | Key management requires lifecycle controls, metadata protection, access control, inventory, and operational discipline. | It does not certify this project's key custody or recovery process. |
| [OpenZeppelin TimelockController documentation](https://docs.openzeppelin.com/contracts/4.x/api/governance) | Timelocks are a practitioner pattern for delaying privileged changes and creating a response window. | Smart-contract timelocks do not establish constitutional legitimacy or prove that 180 days is the right window. |

---

## Abuse Model

| Abuse path | What the actor wants | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Direct file edit | Change a Tier 1 value without using the amendment protocol. | Tests whether startup checks and public hashes catch unauthorized modification. |
| Publication-channel split | Publish different drift-chain heads to different audiences. | Tests whether two-channel verification detects equivocation. |
| Keyholder capture | Coerce or reward enough holders to sign a hostile change. | Tests whether dispersion, disclosure, and timelock response work. |
| Keyholder blockade | Capture three holders to block needed improvements. | Tests whether protection becomes paralysis. |
| Software bypass | Ship a component that ignores the startup check. | Tests whether implementation binding has real accreditation teeth. |
| Supply-chain compromise | Distribute binaries that report compliant hashes while running different logic. | Tests reproducible builds, signature separation, and operator verification. |
| Emergency override pressure | Ask operators to skip checks during crisis. | Tests whether "temporary" exceptions become a drift route. |
| Timelock normalization | Pass a hostile lawful amendment and wait out the public response window. | Tests whether exit, public challenge, and Ombuds reports are meaningful. |
| Monitoring-method drift | Quietly change monitoring fields, access roles, retention, dashboard methods, suppression rules, vendor processors, or raw-log handling while leaving the public claim unchanged. | Tests whether administrative monitoring power can expand without a formal patch, register update, or public challenge path. |

---

## Required Audit Sequence

### Stage 0 - Coverage Inventory

List every file, parameter, article section, annex section, generated artifact, and runtime component that should be covered by the drift layer.

Failure criterion: any Tier 1 rule affects operation but is absent from the covered set or cannot be traced to a protected commitment.

Monitoring coverage must include the Monitoring Purpose Register, dashboard method files, raw-access standards, retention schedules, suppression rules, cleared-flag rules, vendor processing terms, and any code or procedure that controls person-linkable monitoring data.

### Stage 1 - Reproducible Hash Test

Independent reviewers must reproduce the canonical serialization and state hash from a clean checkout.

Failure criterion: two honest reviewers cannot reproduce the same hash using the published rules.

### Stage 2 - Append-Only Log Test

Publish a simulated version change and require monitors to verify prior hash, new state hash, signatures, amendment bundle hash, and consistency with earlier history.

Failure criterion: a changed prior row can be accepted without visible chain breakage.

### Stage 3 - Multi-Channel Equivocation Test

Serve different drift-chain heads on different publication channels and verify that bound components refuse affected operations.

Failure criterion: a component accepts one channel silently or operates through channel divergence without alert.

For monitoring surfaces, the test also serves divergent Monitoring Purpose Register rows, retention clocks, suppression rules, or dashboard methods. A monitoring component fails if it accepts a broader purpose, longer retention period, more permissive raw-access role, or weaker suppression rule without a visible drift event.

### Stage 4 - Startup Refusal Test

**Required input:** A completed Stage 0 Coverage Inventory listing all Tier 1-protected files and bound components. Stage 4 cannot produce a meaningful pass/fail result without this inventory. If Stage 0 has not been completed, Stage 4 must be deferred and recorded as BLOCKED in the audit log.

**Expected sources for the coverage inventory:**
- `architecture/drift_chain.md` — canonical list of Tier 1-protected files and their hash roots
- `docs/governance/Parameter_Calibration_Register.md` — parameters whose values are Tier 1 protected (see "Binding Condition" column: entries bound by Tier 1 or FC-110/FC-111)
- `docs/constitution/INVARIANTS.md` — invariants that bound components must enforce at startup
- Deployment manifest (produced at activation) — list of bound components and their startup verification hooks

**Test procedure:** For each Tier 1-protected file listed in the Stage 0 inventory, modify its content locally (without following the amendment protocol), then start each bound component listed in the inventory.

Failure criterion: any Tier 1-dependent component operates on the modified state without raising the required alert, except the CSM last-verified-state exception (which allows the survival floor to continue operating on the last verified state during a detected drift event).

### Stage 5 - Key-Custody Review

Audit holder identity, dispersion, conflict disclosures, key storage, rotation, emergency rotation, succession, and coercion reporting.

Failure criterion: holder concentration, undisclosed affiliation, or weak key recovery makes 7-of-9 easier to manufacture than the design assumes.

### Stage 6 - Timelock Response Drill

Simulate a hostile but procedurally valid Tier 1 amendment entering the 180-day window.

Required outputs:

- Ombuds integrity report;
- public comment archive;
- counter-amendment path;
- exit notice rehearsal;
- plain-language public explanation;
- holder revocation test;
- final residual-risk statement.

Failure criterion: the public response window exists on paper but affected communities cannot understand, challenge, exit, or organize before effect.

### Stage 7 - Supply-Chain Bypass Test

Attempt to ship a non-compliant implementation that skips drift checks or reports false compliance.

Failure criterion: the implementation can become accredited, deployed, or widely trusted without reproducible build verification and independent code review.

---

## Minimum Evidence Before Stronger Claim

The project may strengthen its claim only after producing:

- covered-state inventory;
- reproducible hash report;
- append-only log consistency test;
- publication-channel divergence drill;
- bound-component startup refusal results;
- keyholder custody and conflict audit;
- timelock response drill;
- supply-chain bypass review;
- monitoring-method drift drill covering register changes, retention changes, access-role changes, dashboard suppression changes, vendor additions, and raw-log handling;
- residual-risk update in the Claims and Evidence Register.

---

## Decision Rule

The drift layer remains **designed mechanism, needs implementation audit** unless unauthorized change is reliably detected, valid change is publicly reviewable, and non-compliant software cannot become accredited without visible exception.

If any stage fails, the failure becomes a Hardening Queue item and the affected architecture file must be revised before the project claims Tier 1 drift is controlled.

---

## Pre-Succession Drift Signal Requirements

**Purpose:** The most consequential drift scenario is not technical misconfiguration — it is a legitimate successor government that inherits the system and gradually hollows it out while maintaining formal compliance. Drift detection must include signals that fire before hollowing becomes irreversible, not after.

**Required signals — all must be active and publishing before any government succession handoff is certified as complete:**

**Signal S1 — Oracle node count:**
The oracle network must meet or exceed FC-030 minimum (5 nodes per cohort) on the day of succession. If oracle count has dropped below minimum at any point in the 90 days before succession, the Implementation Drift Audit Package flags this as a T-022 Administrative Hollowing precursor event, and the Constitutional Integrity Panel (CIP, Annex AM §AM8) is automatically notified.

**Signal S2 — Post-mortem publication continuity:**
The public post-mortem publication schedule must show no gap exceeding 30 days in the 180 days before succession. A gap triggers an automatic CIP review under the AM3 hollowing triggers. The Capture Dashboard must show the post-mortem publication record as a real-time metric.

**Signal S3 — Founding institution seat vacancy:**
No founding institution seat (Federated Ombuds, Constitutional Integrity Panel, oracle adversarial seat) may be vacant for >90 days as of the succession date. Vacancies exceeding 90 days as of succession day are flagged as potential T-022 administrative capture and logged in the drift audit before the successor government takes office.

**Signal S4 — Tier 1 hash integrity:**
The cryptographic hash of all Tier 1 documents (Humane Constitution, INVARIANTS, Founding Commitments Tier 1 rows) must be verified against the bound reference hashes and published in the drift audit within 7 days before succession. Any hash mismatch is a succession blocker — succession cannot be certified until the mismatch is explained and resolved.

**Signal S5 — Enforcement Panel publication record:**
The Enforcement Panel must have published at least 4 Annual Deterrence Audits (one per year) if the system has been running for 4+ years, or one per year of operation. A gap in the publication record before succession is logged as a T-022 precursor. The new government inherits the publication obligation with no grace period.

**Signal S6 — Essential Access continuity confirmation:**
On the day of succession, an independent confirmation (not self-reported by the outgoing government) must verify that Essential Access issuance and delivery are operating above the CSM floor for all enrolled persons. If any category is below floor, succession is not blocked but the status is published with the succession record and triggers a mandatory 30-day continuity review by the Constitutional Integrity Panel.

**Succession certification block:** Signals S1 through S4 are hard blocks — succession is not certified until each signal is confirmed green. Signals S5 and S6 are soft flags — succession proceeds, but the flags are published and the CIP begins a mandatory 90-day review.

**Responsibility:** The drift audit for succession purposes is conducted by the Constitutional Integrity Panel (Annex AM §AM8), not by the outgoing or incoming government. The incoming government receives a copy of the audit findings on day 1 of office.

---

## Residual Risk

Tamper evidence is not anti-corruption magic. It raises the cost of hidden change; it does not make holders virtuous, operators honest, or lawful hostile amendment impossible. The strongest honest claim is narrower: quiet drift should become visible fast enough that people can challenge, exit, or restore the last valid state.

docs.title: Parameter Calibration Register
docs.summary: This register tracks high-risk parameters whose numbers matter enough that they should not remain "because the document says so."
docs.content:
# Parameter Calibration Register

This register tracks high-risk parameters whose numbers matter enough that they should not remain "because the document says so."

It complements `/founding/commitments.md` and `architecture/parameter_registry.md`. Those files bind values. This file records why values are plausible, how they should be tested, and what evidence would force revision.

---

## Row Standard

Every high-risk parameter should eventually have:

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Parameter | FC identifier or named constitutional threshold. |
| Current value | Bound, provisional, reserved, or proposed value. |
| Tier | Tier 1, Tier 2, Tier 3, or pre-launch blocking gate. |
| Why this value exists | Plain-language rationale, not just cross-reference. |
| Capture route | How a bad actor benefits from this value being too high, too low, vague, or stale. |
| Evidence needed | Simulation, pilot, audit, legal review, or outside source needed. |
| Revision trigger | What result forces review. |
| Governing docs | Where the value is bound and where consequences appear. |

---

## Seed Register

| Parameter | Current value | Tier | Why this value exists | Capture route | Evidence needed | Revision trigger | Governing docs |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| FC-010 leakage thresholds | 3% routine target; 7% systemic review | Tier 1 / active-unproven | Converts shadow-convertibility enforcement capacity into published thresholds. | Too high normalizes broker markets; too low justifies surveillance. | Proxy-market red team; enforcement-capacity audit. | Broker profitability remains positive or ordinary households face overbroad enforcement. | Annex AB; Threat Register T-001. |
| FC-020 Protected Pause floor | 0.30 Voice | Tier 1 | Prevents hardship from erasing civic continuity. | Too high preserves incumbents; too low punishes illness and care. | Hardship-pause abuse test. | Pause rings preserve roles or genuine hardship users avoid protection. | Annex AF; Service Record Misuse Evidence Test Package. |
| FC-030 oracle N_MIN | 5 nodes | Tier 1 | Prevents single-source measurement control. | Too low enables capture; too high causes paralysis. | Capacity measurement and oracle-failure drill. | Quorum failure blocks response or correlated error persists. | Annex AL; Capacity Measurement Evidence Test Package. |
| FC-031 methodology classes | 3 classes | Tier 1 | Reduces shared epistemic failure. | Standards bodies define all classes in their own image. | Methodology-class audit. | Method classes share data, funding, model supply chain, or standards body. | Annex AL; T-020/T-021. |
| FC-032 pairwise correlation max | 0.30 Pearson, supplemented by directional-bias review | Tier 1 | Makes formal independence testable while preventing same-direction bias from passing merely because co-movement is low. | Actors tune reporting to pass correlation while sharing assumptions or incentives that push all errors in the same direction. | Forecast-vs-actual, directional-bias table, and oracle independence audit. | Shared error exceeds threshold during stress, or same-direction material error recurs without corrective action. | Annex AL; T-020/T-021. |
| FC-033 adversarial oracle seats | 1 per cohort | Tier 1 | Forces hostile-method review inside measurement. | Seat becomes symbolic or captured by same professional network. | Adversarial-seat independence review. | Dissent never changes outcomes or is not resourced. | Annex AL; T-020/T-021. |
| FC-040 penalty base multiplier | 5.0x | Tier 1 / active-unproven | Makes detected conversion expected-value negative. | Too low invites arbitrage; too high punishes edge cases harshly. | Annual deterrence audit. | Violations remain profitable or appeals show disproportionate harm. | Annex AJ; T-001/T-007. |
| FC-041 detection assumption | 0.85 | Tier 1 / active-unproven | Supplies expected-value calculation for penalties. | Overstated detection hides under-enforcement. | Detection-rate audit. | Actual detection probability materially lower than assumed. | Annex AJ; Enforcement Panel audit. |
